meta.name: Asthma Triggered by Allergic Reaction — Overlapping Physiology

patient.name: Devin Ross
patient.chiefComplaint: Acute severe wheezing and hives after cat exposure

history.hpi: 30-year-old male with severe allergic asthma (prior ICU admission, never intubated). Visiting a friend's house and didn't realize they had cats until he was already inside. Symptoms started about 20 minutes after arrival with wheezing and hives that have progressed rapidly. Used his rescue inhaler 4 times with minimal improvement.
history.events: Known cat allergy. Didn't know friend had cats. Symptoms began 20 minutes after arrival, rapidly progressing over the last 10 minutes.

scene.dispatch: Medic 6, respond code 3 to 3620 Ashton Circle for a 30-year-old male with difficulty breathing, possible allergic reaction.
scene.location: Single-family home, living room. Patient on the couch. Two cats visible.
scene.safety: Scene safe — move patient outside to the front porch to minimize ongoing allergen exposure during treatment.
scene.bystanders: Friend who provides history and retrieves the patient's belongings
visualCues: Young adult male sitting on the couch leaning forward, visibly distressed
Spreading urticaria on the forearms, neck, and face
Audible expiratory wheezing from across the room
Used albuterol inhaler on the coffee table
Lips slightly swollen but tongue appears normal

phases.name: Arrival — Asthma + Allergic Reaction Overlap
phases.description: Patient has overlapping bronchospasm (asthma) and distributive/mucocutaneous features (allergic reaction). Meets criteria for anaphylaxis (respiratory compromise + skin involvement from a known allergen). IM epinephrine is first-line and doubles as a bronchodilator. Also treat the asthma component aggressively.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent; mild lip swelling but no tongue swelling; no stridor
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Tachypneic at 36/min with diffuse expiratory wheezes bilaterally; prolonged expiratory phase; accessory muscle use

skin.color: flushed with diffuse urticaria on face, neck, chest, arms

otherFindings: Meets anaphylaxis criteria (respiratory compromise + skin findings + known allergen exposure)
Known severe asthma increases risk for deterioration
Patient is cooperative and able to hold CPAP if needed

expectedActions.action: Move patient outside to the front porch or to a cat-free room to minimize ongoing allergen exposure
Administer Epinephrine 1 mg/mL (1:1,000) 0.3 mg IM in the lateral thigh — treats BOTH the allergic reaction and the bronchospasm via beta-2 effect
expectedActions.rationale: This patient meets anaphylaxis criteria. IM epinephrine is first-line and its bronchodilator effect also treats the asthma component.

expectedActions.action: Apply high-flow oxygen via NRB while preparing nebulizer
Administer Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized; repeat albuterol as needed
Administer Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV OR Dexamethasone 10 mg IV — treats both asthma and allergic components
Administer Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV for urticaria and histamine response
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography (expect shark fin waveform)

phases.name: Default — Rapid Improvement with Epi and Bronchodilators
phases.description: IM epinephrine and nebulized bronchodilators take effect quickly. Urticaria stabilizes, wheezing decreases, SpO2 improves. Transport with continued observation for biphasic reaction.
phases.triggerCondition: Improvement after IM epi and bronchodilators

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Less wheezing, decreased work of breathing, RR decreasing
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'That shot helped a lot... I can breathe better'

expectedActions.action: Transport to ED for observation — biphasic anaphylaxis can occur 2-8 hours later, especially in asthmatics
Continuous monitoring for recurrence of wheezing, hypotension, or urticaria
Counsel the patient to get an updated Epi-pen and avoid the trigger; document that current auto-injector is expired

phases.name: Branch: Progression to Full Anaphylaxis with Respiratory Failure
phases.description: Crew delays epinephrine and focuses only on bronchodilators. Patient progresses to full anaphylaxis with hypotension and worsening bronchospasm. Requires escalation to IV fluids, epinephrine drip, and magnesium sulfate for severe bronchospasm.

otherFindings: Progression to full anaphylaxis from delayed epinephrine
Now meets criteria for aggressive resuscitation

expectedActions.action: Administer Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM immediately; may repeat in 5 minutes
Administer NS 20 mL/kg (1600 mL for 80 kg patient) IV bolus rapidly
Administer Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 10-15 minutes for severe bronchospasm
Prepare Epinephrine IV drip at 0.5 mcg/kg/min (40 mcg/min) for refractory anaphylaxis
Prepare for RSI if patient deteriorates further; ketamine is the induction agent of choice for bronchospasm

criticalActions: Recognize anaphylaxis criteria: respiratory compromise + skin findings + known allergen exposure
Move patient away from ongoing allergen
Administer Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM (1 mg/mL) as first-line — treats BOTH components
Administer Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized with repetition
Administer Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV or Dexamethasone 10 mg IV
expectedActions: Continuous cardiac and waveform capnography monitoring
Educate patient on trigger avoidance and updated Epi-pen
NS fluid bolus if BP drops
bonusActions: Articulate that epi is the single most effective intervention (treats both allergic and asthmatic components)
Recognize biphasic anaphylaxis risk in asthmatics
Prepare for magnesium and RSI contingencies

learningObjectives: Identify overlapping asthma and allergic reaction physiology
Apply MATC Anaphylaxis protocol when criteria are met
Understand epinephrine's dual role as bronchodilator and anti-allergic
Manage both components without treating only the 'asthma' piece
discussionQuestions: How does epinephrine treat both the asthma and allergic components?
What criteria define anaphylaxis and does this patient meet them?
Why is observation critical after symptoms resolve?
How would your approach differ in a patient with severe asthma alone versus asthma with allergic overlay?
When would you escalate to magnesium sulfate?
commonPitfalls: Only treating the wheezing and missing the anaphylaxis diagnosis
Delaying IM epinephrine in favor of nebulized albuterol alone
Forgetting that urticaria + wheezing + allergen = anaphylaxis
Allowing the patient to refuse transport after symptom improvement
Using an expired epi auto-injector dose (patient's own was expired)
keyTakeaways: Epi is the drug that treats both components — give it early
Anaphylaxis criteria are broader than people remember
Biphasic reactions happen hours later — transport every patient
Severe asthma history is a mortality predictor
